hero.question: Will Rafael López Aliaga win the 2026 Peruvian presidential election?
hero.prob_take: At 7.5%, the crowd has essentially written off López Aliaga, betting 92.5% that someone else wins Peru's 2026 race, which means you're getting paid for tail risk that assumes zero path to victory for a candidate who actually polled competitive just months ago. The real question is whether his recent corruption allegations and right-wing positioning are permanent disqualifiers or temporary noise in a volatile Peruvian political cycle, but the market's pricing suggests zero comeback probability, which feels overcorrected given how fast Peru's politics can swing.

daily_take.headline: López Aliaga Tanks 18 Points: Peru's Right-Wing Bet Just Got Way Cheaper
daily_take.deck: Peru's former far-right candidate collapsed 18 points overnight on Polymarket, now sitting at just 7.5%. The move signals real weakness in his ground game or internal polling that hasn't hit news yet. At these odds he's either dead money or severely mispriced, but the volume ($5.1M) means smart money is already pricing in a comeback story that's not coming.

sidebar.headline: Peace deal odds jumped 14 points as Iran sanctions rhetoric cooled. Market's pricing in Biden admin scrambling before April 22, but permanent deals take years, not months.
sidebar.label: US x Iran Peace Deal: 24.5%

sidebar.headline: Lightning got 11 points of support in 24 hours. Sharp money likes the underdog value, or one side just got sharper injury intel than the other.
sidebar.label: Rangers vs. Lightning: 47.5%

sidebar.headline: Hormuz traffic returning to normal nearly flat at 24.5%, but the $8.9M volume suggests sophisticated hedging, not conviction. Supply chain players are protecting downside, not betting on resolution.
sidebar.label: Strait of Hormuz Normalization: 24.5%
